sentences.text: Good afternoon, Brad.
Yes, Renata Adler, R-E-N-A-T-A, and Adler, A-D-L-E-R.
It was written with an E on the end, Renate Adler.
I was born in Hamburg, July 22, 1925.
It was really a normal childhood for a few years until-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I can't stop right now-- until things became difficult for Jewish people to live.
I went to school and had a normal childhood, as I said before, with most of my relatives.
My father died when I was very small.
So I had plenty of uncles and aunts, grandparents until '38, when I was thrown out of school and I could not go to school any further.
My father's name was Max Edward Adler.
He was a lawyer.
Oh, yes, many of them.
He was a very tall man and very humorous, quite a sense of humor.
He took me for walks.
And when I was away with my mother, he would come for the weekend and visit with us.
Then he had to go back to the city.
And otherwise, it was normal.
Of course, you know, like every child thinks, their father's the best.
Almost 4.
Of course, I understood that my father wasn't there any more.
Difficult for me was to see the effect it had on my mother, and as it was custom to go in black, completely black.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Her sadness and all that was, I think, made great impression on me.
I think I, myself, was dressed in white, which was a custom at the time, for about two months, then they gave up on it.
I got too dirty.
And so it was a hard year until she finally, finally got used to the fact that her husband was dead.
My mother's name is Gerda.
A very charming lady, who was always busy doing something.
She ran the household where we lived.
It was a large apartment.
And she was busy with the family, her parents, her sister, and whoever else was there.
Like, you know, a mother.
She saw to it that I did my homework, that I did my piano lessons, and stuff like that.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes, very much.
Oh, I liked to play Chopin and Brahms.
Unfortunately, I don't play anymore.
Tchaikovsky was also very good.
Oh, yes.
She was a concert pianist before she got married.
The first one was a private school, then it was a half-private school.
The second one was called Milburg, and they were closed at the time then.
And I was put into an absolute state school or public school.
And I must say, the people there were very nice, even though they knew I was Jewish, and they-- neither did they care nor did they make remarks about me.
I was just one of them.
So came Kristallnacht, and I couldn't go to school anymore.
I was really sad because I enjoyed going to school.
Yeah, I always liked languages.
I spoke French quite well, which I've forgotten in the meantime.
And sports, and also the usual ones.
I just enjoy it.
No.
I was always the only Jewish one in the whole school.
In the second school, Milburg, I was affected by a teacher, who absolutely hated Jews and did everything to discourage me.
Gave me bad notes and reprimanded me for things I never did, which, in the other school, the last one, I never realized.
People were especially nice to me and made no difference between me and other children.
Those were friends from my mother or from the family.
Some of them were Jewish, some of them were not.
And it didn't make any difference.
And I still know a lady, or girl, or whatever you want to call it.
I went to school with her at Milburg.
And we are still friendly.
Whenever I go to Europe, I see her.
I think it started in [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] '32, you know, before Hitler came to power.
But they had already the inkling that Jews were not the best people.
And so, sometimes, people would make remarks in a store or whatever.
Like, oh, the little Jewish girl.
But that's not all.
Until '33, of course, things changed rapidly.
People took a stand, whether they just took a stand as to not be punished by others, or what, but there were people who didn't want to have anything to do with us anymore.
And people who [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] were especially nice, which is very difficult for a child to understand.
And my mother had an awful lot of explaining to do.
My mother remarried the day when we were transplanted to Theresienstadt.
She married then Dr.
Rudolphi, who was a judge, but he got pensioned off very early.
And he did an awful lot of work for the Jewish community.

And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] since he had a daughter from a former marriage who was half Jewish, he had not been in any danger that he needed to be transported or deported, which, of course, the minute when he married my mother, that was alleviated.
So they married one day before we came to Theresienstadt.
I only know that-- for once-- once, she said to me, always be proud of what you are and a leopard doesn't change its spots, so neither can you.
And we are Jewish, and that's all there is to it, and you might as well be proud of it.
And that was the attitude I got, very simple.
Yeah, later, during the war.
Not before, no.
More of them the end of '30s, not in the mid-30s.
Well, we had to get-- they confiscated an awful lot of our possessions.
They confiscated all the jewelry, all the gold, silver, and platinum.
You only could have one place setting, that's all.
All the furs, radios.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That is what I can see was in my household.
But I do know that people, their bank accounts were confiscated, their possessions, their real estate was confiscated.
But since I didn't know whether we had any or not, or my mother, so I was-- I had no idea about that until I heard it later.
But around me, in my house, I only knew that things had to be sorted out, silver and stuff like that, silver and gold and platinum.
Which some of that we got back after the war, believe it or not.
No.
No, but my mother's.
I believe it was a pension from his law office, and my grandfather helped out.
And that's all what I know.
Children were not told that much about financial matters at the time.
My mother's father, right.
My grandfather?
Oh, yes, I was.
His name was Felix Schoenfeld.
And I always say that I've learned just as much from him that my mother learned from him in different ways.
I loved him dearly.
He was very strict and very loving.
And he always had time to explain things to me, and he always knew what to explain and how to explain things.
And he was just delightful.
My grandmother was alive.
And she was equally caring and very supportive.
They were people who just decided that they had just another daughter suddenly since my mother was without a husband.
So they treated me like another daughter.
Very little.
I knew very little about the religion per se, except grandparents went to temple on the high holidays.
And Grandfather and a few other people who had the money built a new temple because they found it was necessary.
Yeah.
It's still there, but it's used now for something else, recording, as a recording studio, as far as I know.
Say it again, the question.
That was all.
I didn't know very much about the religion until I met a friend of mine whose father was a rabbi.
And she walked with me for two or three years through each day and explained all the holidays.
And it was an education, which I had no idea.
It was a real education.
Not in my, house but in their house, yeah.
My mother was not-- was not there.
She was on a trip.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She couldn't even telephone me.
And I was at home alone with a maid.
And I remember some man or friends of my mother came in and wanted to have refuge because everybody knew that there was no man in the house.
So they decided if they come to my house, they will not be-- nobody will look for them.
They asked me and I said, well, I could understand that and I just let them hide in my apartment.
In closets.
No.
In my apartment.
Well, I heard what was going on in the streets.
And Grandfather called and said not to go out in the street and tried to assure me that everything would be all right.
And Grandmother called and told me the same thing.
And just that, you know, you have to live through it.
That's life, very, very strict.
No, nobody came to the house at the time.
Two days.
Kristallnacht?
Next morning, I had a teacher in the school, and the teacher called me.
And he was an officer, I think, in the Luftwaffe or something, Air Force, on the side.
And he said, he was very sorry that I couldn't-- but I shouldn't come to school anymore.
He would come by later to explain things to me.
But he didn't want me to even venture out of the house.
He was a very caring man, a Dr.
Suter, never forget him.
He did, of course.
He told me from what had happened and that he was very sorry.
And he asked where my mother was.
I said she isn't there.
So he was kind of surprised that I had to live through all this by myself.
And I said, well, I had the maid there.
Nobody's doing me any harm.
What do you want?
So he kind of said he would come back and he came back every two days to check up on me, whether I was all right.
What else did you observe?
Did you go out at all the day after Kristallnacht?
No, no, I was told not to go out, so I didn't.
I heard that all the windows were broken and people were being arrested and the Gestapo was very busy.
And these men in my apartment, they were afraid that the Gestapo would get them.
But they were well taken care of in the meantime.
So they only left, you know, two or three days later, well-fed.
Oh, yes.
There was a large store called [NON-ENGLISH].
This was a store for-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a department store, similar to Saks Fifth Avenue.
It was completely ruined.
And those were friends of ours.
I know that in another-- in another part of Germany, where the religious Jews were living, everything was broken.
That was terrible.
You know, people told me, called me, told me.
Yeah, they were OK because he was older and they didn't bother him at all.
She came back about four or five days later.
She, in the meantime, was in Baden-Baden, I believe it was, and they were put all together into a synagogue with hundreds and hundreds of people in Baden-Baden.
And then they set fire to the synagogue just to scare the people.
And then they extinguished the fire, but they just wanted to scare them.
Yes, she came home.
Oh.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The director of all schools in Hamburg, who was an acquaintance of ours, saw to it that I got a lady, her name was Dr.
Liese.
And she gave me private lessons until '39, so as much as I could absorb within a very short time.
And she was rather strict, but it was OK.
And I had to go to her house five hours a day and I had to continue all my schooling.
Maybe a half an hour walk or so.
Many people emigrated.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] See, people did emigrate earlier, but they could take all their possessions.
Now, people were thinking they wanted to emigrate, but then they couldn't take as many possessions anymore.
And then, they could just take the jewelry and a few things out of the house or their apartments.
And so it was a constant goodbye.
People were emigrating to the States or wherever, whoever was the quota the largest where they could get into, in which country.
Yeah, I believe that my grandfather had plans, but all this went away.
I don't know.
I only know that Grandfather had made plans, but something very unfortunate happened.
And my mother's brother-in-law fled the country, and as punishment for that, we could not cross the borders.
Yeah, he left illegally and then my grandfather was thrown into prison and came out again, but he could never leave the country under any circumstances.
When the Germans realized that my uncle had fled the country and diverted some money.
My grandfather?
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I wasn't allowed to visit him in prison.
He didn't want that.
My mother was able to see him and his wife was able to see him.
I remember, when he came back, he looked very shopworn, to say the least, and was very tired.
He didn't speak much about it.
He was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] aghast about what had happened to him on account of his son-in-law.
I think it worked very hard on his mind, particularly that he realized that we could not leave the country and whatever was coming was coming and we could not get away.
Only about six weeks.
No, he was not allowed to write, not to use the telephone.
He just could have, you know, his wife or my mother come to see him.
No, it was in the middle of the city.
It was in Hamburg, in the middle of the city.
I cannot.
I could take you there.
I don't know the name.
That was in beginning of '39.
Yeah.
Deeply, in a way, because I knew that he had given so much trust and whatever you want to call it to his son-in-law.
And that this man betrayed him to such extent.
I felt so bad for Grandfather.
I'm sure you would understand that.
I never forgave my uncle for it.
I think I saw him after the war twice.
Once to give him a piece of my mind and I said, I will never put my feet under the same table with you again, and I didn't.
OK.
